prompt: A Scholarly Resources & Teaching Operations management system that includes end-to-end support for library serials, course resources, and onsite engagement analytics in a fully offline environment. Users are separated into System Administrator, Library Staff, Academic Scheduler, Instructor, and Auditor roles, each seeing only the navigation and data permitted to them. The Dioxus-based web UI provides fast, form-driven workflows for creating and maintaining journal records and teaching resource master data, with clear save states, inline validation, and a visible “effective version” selector so staff can review prior versions without overwriting the current record. Library Staff can attach locally stored files such as sample issues, contracts, and vendor quotes, preview common formats, and see a checksum confirmation after upload to detect accidental file changes. Academic Schedulers and Instructors maintain course catalogs and sections, including prerequisites, credit/contact hours, capacity, and time/location notes, and can bulk import/export via CSV or Excel templates with a dry-run mode that reports row-level errors before committing. For in-person participation, staff and instructors run a one-tap check-in screen that confirms success within one second, warns on suspected duplicates, and allows a single retry with an explicit reason selection. Dashboards present operational and learning-adjacent metrics such as course popularity, fill rate, drop rate, instructor workload, foot traffic, dwell time, and interaction quality, with configurable date filters and role-based masking so sensitive instructor notes and student identifiers are never shown to unauthorized viewers. Scheduled reports run locally and are delivered as downloadable files from the reporting page at the configured time (for example, every Monday at 7:00 AM), with exports restricted by the viewer’s department scope. The backend uses Rocket to expose decoupled REST-style APIs consumed by the Dioxus frontend over the local network or localhost, with all authentication performed offline using username/password and salted password hashing (minimum 12 characters required, lockout after 5 failed attempts for 15 minutes). MySQL provides persistent storage for master data, version history, attachments metadata, check-in evidence, metric definitions, and audit logs; attachments themselves are stored on local disk with paths and hashes recorded in MySQL. Journal and course records are versioned on every approved edit, retaining at least 24 months of history, with an optional “publish” action that marks a version as the operational baseline. Check-in validation enforces a configurable time window (default 10 minutes), blocks duplicates for the same user/session within that window, records device fingerprint attributes available to the browser, and optionally enforces a local network rule (for example, must be on an approved SSID name entered by an admin) without relying on external geolocation services. The metrics semantic layer is stored as versioned definitions with lineage so derived metrics can reference base metrics and window calculations consistently across dashboards; changing a definition requires an admin review and automatically flags dependent charts for verification. Audit logging is immutable at the application layer, capturing logins, permission changes, circulation-style actions, data edits, imports/exports, and report generation; logs are searchable by user, object, and time range, exportable to CSV, and protected with tamper detection using chained hashes. A retention policy is configurable (default 7 years for audit logs, 3 years for operational events), with secure deletion applied on expiry, and sensitive fields are encrypted at rest where appropriate and masked in responses unless the requesting role is explicitly authorized.
